methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Seed → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.5x) to $10M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 4 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in Defense & Security. Range: $60M - $500M.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Defense & Dual-use
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Defense & Dual-use

private.name: Tenna Systems
private.rationale: Same sector (Defense & Security), same stage (Seed), post-money $60M

data.narrative:
Andrenam is a seed-stage defense and security company founded by Matej Cernosek and Alex Chu that has raised $10M to date. The valuation analysis draws on a comparative universe of 10 private peers and 2 public companies operating in the Defense & Security sector, including Tenna Systems, Anduril Industries, Scale AI, Saronic, and Flock Safety.

Two triangulation methods were applied. Stage-progression analysis uses the $10M total raised as a base proxy and applies a 2.5x multiplier typical of Seed to next-stage advancement, implying a $25M valuation. Peer-median analysis across four same-sector, same/adjacent-stage comparables—Stark Defence ($500M Series A), Vatn Systems ($300M Series A), Ares Industries ($150M Series A), and Tenna Systems ($60M Seed)—yields a median of $225M, with a range spanning $60M to $500M.

The two methods produce a wide implied range: $21M on the conservative end, $125M at the median, and $259M at the high end. The significant divergence reflects tension between stage-appropriate valuation mechanics and peer-set positioning. Stage-progression suggests Andrenam commands a modest premium to deployed capital. Peer median is materially higher but incorporates Series A stage companies and may reflect sector momentum rather than seed-stage comparability. Key uncertainties include the appropriate peer weighting given the stage gap between Tenna Systems (Seed, $60M) and the Series A cohort, and whether Andrenam's specific technical or market positioning justifies deviation from stage-median benchmarks.
